subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Americans Who Believed Climate Change Would Pose Serious Threat, 1997–2015
stimulus: Source: Gallup, October 2021. Line graph showing the Percentage of Americans Who Believed Climate Change Would Pose a Serious Threat in Their Lifetime from 1997 to 2015, showing an overall increase with a dip around 2010.

parts.prompt: Identify the percentage of Americans in 2010 who believed that climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime, according to the data in the line graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Acceptable answers: 32% is the precise answer, but accept any value between 31% and 34% inclusive due to the precision of reading a line graph.

scoring_points.description: Identify the percentage of Americans in 2010 who believed that climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime, according to the data in the line graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies approximately 32% (acceptable range: 31%–34% inclusive) as the percentage of Americans in 2010 who believed climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Identifies approximately 32% (acceptable range: 31%–34% inclusive) as the percentage of Americans in 2010 who believed climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime.

wrong_examples: About 40%. (Outside the acceptable range — 40% would correspond to a different year on the graph; 2010 shows the lowest recent value)
The percentage is high in 2010. (Does not provide a specific numerical value — identification questions require a specific number or range)
common_traps: 2010 represents a local low point (trough) on the graph — students may assume the question is asking about a high point; read the specific year carefully
AP readers accept a small range for reading line graphs — an answer between 31% and 34% earns full credit; do not be overly precise
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the percentage of Americans in 2010 who believed climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime, according to the line graph. Acceptable response: 32%. Scoring notes: answers between 31% and 34% (inclusive) are also acceptable.

sample_responses.response: In 2010, approximately 32% of Americans believed that climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime.
sample_responses.commentary: Exact CB value (32%). Earns the point.
Within the acceptable 31%–34% range for line-graph reading. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Around 45% of Americans believed climate change was a serious threat in 2010.
sample_responses.commentary: Outside the 31%–34% acceptable range — 2010 was a low point on the graph, not a high point. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Describe a trend in the data shown in the line graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate trend description supported by the data. Only one trend needs to be described. Either the overall upward trend or the dip around 2010 earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Describe a trend in the data shown in the line graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Describes a trend in the data. Accept any ONE of the following: the percentage of Americans who feel climate change would pose a serious threat increased over the period shown; the percentage increased from 1997 to 2008; from 2008 to 2010, the percentage declined.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Describes a trend in the data. Accept any ONE of the following: the percentage of Americans who feel climate change would pose a serious threat increased over the period shown; the percentage increased from 1997 to 2008; from 2008 to 2010, the percentage declined.

wrong_examples: About 32% of Americans believed in climate change in 2010. (Describes a single data point, not a trend — a trend must show direction over time)
Americans are worried about climate change. (Vague generalization not grounded in the specific data — does not describe a direction of change over the period shown)
common_traps: A trend requires showing change over time — identifying a single data point does not earn the point; use language like 'increased,' 'decreased,' or 'fluctuated' to show a trend
Only one trend is needed — do not try to describe every feature of the graph; just identify one clear directional pattern
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing a trend in the data shown in the line graph. Acceptable responses include: the percentage of Americans who feel climate change would pose a serious threat increased over the period shown; the percentage of Americans who believed climate change would pose a serious threat to them increased from 1997 to 2008; from 2008 to 2010, the percentage of Americans who believed climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetimes declined.

sample_responses.response: Overall, the percentage of Americans who feel that climate change would pose a serious threat in their lifetime has increased over the period from 1997 to 2015.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the overall upward trend across the full time period — CB-accepted description. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: From 2008 to 2010, the percentage of Americans who believed climate change would pose a serious threat declined.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a valid directional sub-trend (the short-term decline). Only one trend is needed. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: About 32% of Americans believed climate change was a threat in 2010.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a single data point rather than a trend — a trend must show direction over time. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about how a trend in the line graph could be used by an interest group to influence policymaking.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must go beyond describing the trend to drawing a conclusion about how an interest group could strategically use that trend. Accept conclusions from either an environmentalist or anti-regulation perspective.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about how a trend in the line graph could be used by an interest group to influence policymaking.

required_elements: 1 pt: Draws a conclusion about interest group strategy based on the data. Accept: interest groups could see an upward trend in those concerned about climate change and lobby Congress to pass policies responding to those concerns; since the percentage worried about the environment is trending upward, an environmental interest group could engage in an educational campaign to convince voters to pressure their representatives; since the percentage never exceeded 50%, interest groups could pressure government agencies to ease environmental regulations.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Draws a conclusion about interest group strategy based on the data. Accept: interest groups could see an upward trend in those concerned about climate change and lobby Congress to pass policies responding to those concerns; since the percentage worried about the environment is trending upward, an environmental interest group could engage in an educational campaign to convince voters to pressure their representatives; since the percentage never exceeded 50%, interest groups could pressure government agencies to ease environmental regulations.

wrong_examples: Interest groups care about climate change. (Does not use the data to draw a strategic conclusion about how an interest group would act)
The percentage of Americans worried about climate change went up. (Describes the trend without drawing a conclusion about how an interest group would use that information)
common_traps: The conclusion must connect the DATA (the trend) to a specific interest group STRATEGY — describe both the trend and how an interest group would use it as a tool for influencing policymakers
Accept conclusions from either pro-environment groups (citing rising concern) or anti-regulation groups (citing that majority still not convinced) — both use the data differently
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for drawing a conclusion about how a trend in the line graph could be used by an interest group to influence policymaking. Acceptable conclusions: interest groups could see an upward trend in those concerned about climate change and lobby Congress to pass policies responding to those concerns; since the percentage of Americans worried about the environment is trending upward, an environmental interest group could engage in an educational campaign to convince voters to put pressure on their representatives; since the percentage of Americans who felt climate change would pose a threat in their lifetime never exceeded 50%, interest groups could put pressure on government agencies to ease environmental regulations.

sample_responses.response: Because the percentage of Americans concerned about climate change has been trending upward, an environmental interest group like the Sierra Club could use this data to lobby Congress to pass policies responding to climate change, arguing that a growing share of the electorate supports action.
sample_responses.commentary: Draws a strategic conclusion by tying the upward trend to interest-group lobbying strategy. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Since the percentage of Americans who felt climate change would pose a threat never exceeded 50%, an anti-regulation interest group could pressure government agencies to ease environmental regulations by arguing that a majority of Americans do not view climate change as a serious personal threat.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the data strategically from an anti-regulation perspective — both directions are acceptable as long as the conclusion connects the data to an interest group's policy-making strategy. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Interest groups care about climate change policy and try to influence Congress.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not use the data from the line graph — makes a generic statement about interest groups without tying to the specific trend shown. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the overall trend shown in the line graph could be a result of political socialization.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect political socialization (agents of socialization: media, education, family, peers, etc.) to the specific trend in the data. Accept any plausible explanation that identifies an agent of socialization and its effect.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the overall trend shown in the line graph could be a result of political socialization.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains how political socialization could explain the trend. Accept: increased exposure to media reports about climate change might have been one of the factors that increased belief over time; the increase could be a result of changes in educational experiences (schools teaching about climate science); the percentage that never exceeded 40% could be a result of peer or family groups reinforcing beliefs that climate change is not a severe issue.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains how political socialization could explain the trend. Accept: increased exposure to media reports about climate change might have been one of the factors that increased belief over time; the increase could be a result of changes in educational experiences (schools teaching about climate science); the percentage that never exceeded 40% could be a result of peer or family groups reinforcing beliefs that climate change is not a severe issue.

wrong_examples: People learned about climate change on the news. (Partially correct — mentions media but does not make the link to political socialization as a concept; must use the term or describe the process of opinion formation through agents of socialization)

The government changed its position on climate change. (Government position change is not political socialization — socialization refers to how individuals form political opinions through agents like family, school, media, and peers)
common_traps: Political socialization refers to the process by which individuals form political attitudes and beliefs through agents of socialization (family, school, media, peers, religion) — the response must connect a specific agent to the trend
The response must explain CAUSATION: because of [agent of socialization], Americans' beliefs about climate change shifted in the direction shown in the graph
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the overall trend shown in the line graph could be a result of political socialization. Acceptable explanations: increased exposure to media reports about climate change might have been one of the factors that increased the belief over time that climate change is an issue; the increase in the percentage of Americans who believe that climate change is a serious problem could be a result of changes in educational experiences; the overall percentage of Americans who are concerned about climate change never exceeded 40% between 1997 and 2015 which could be a result of peer or family groups reinforcing beliefs amongst their members that climate change is not a severe issue.

sample_responses.response: Increased exposure to media reports about climate change — including news coverage of extreme weather events and scientific findings — might have been one of the factors that increased belief over time that climate change is a serious issue. The media is a major agent of political socialization, shaping attitudes through what information is made available.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific agent of political socialization (media) and explains how increased exposure caused the upward trend shown in the graph. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The percentage of Americans concerned about climate change never exceeded 40%, which could be a result of peer or family groups reinforcing beliefs that climate change is not a severe issue — family is a primary agent of political socialization that forms lasting attitudes.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies family/peers as agents of political socialization and explains the trend (why it stays capped) through that lens. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Americans have always cared about the environment, so it makes sense that the percentage has gone up.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not identify any agent of political socialization or explain the socialization process that produced the trend. 0 pts.
